speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Anita Karl. The date is December 10, 1995. This is the beginning of tape 2. You were telling me about the anti-Semitism that you experienced outside the ghetto when the ghetto's doors were open, and you could come and go during the day. Do you remember anything else besides that incident at the castle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we were to do-- one day we were very late coming back. And we were-- we had to run, because otherwise we would be shot. So my mother-- and we were small children, and she had to carry the baby. So we had to run, and people-- non-Jews would yell, there go the Jews, the Jews, the Jews. And if a German would have seen us, he would have certainly shot us. The anti-Semitism was growing by leaps and bounds, because now there was no one from the Jewish community that could defend us. The world was standing still. There was absolutely no reaction from any other power or any other country that would inquire after us. We were lost in a maze of terror. And we were sport to whoever wanted to harm us, and no one would lift a finger. So fear came in to the Jews, and glory and power visited the Gentiles. So they became strong, and they would harass us and go after us constantly without any measure and without any ending to it. And soon my father got very sick. And even though he was ill and not feeling well, he was taken to the labor camps of [? Janowska. ?] And over there, one day he had to-- his job was to go to the cemetery and take out all the stones, the marbles, of the people that had died so the Germans could pave the streets for their tanks to have a better way of entering. Because in Poland, and in mainly in-- in-- in Europe, the streets were made of stones, and that was not very comfortable for the big trucks that came. So they made [INAUDIBLE]-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot to take the phone off. Oh, I'm so sorry. It's OK. So they-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --they actually paved the street with the marble headstones of the Jews who had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They forced them to do that. And my father hit upon this stone of his son, of Emmanuel, my brother who had died, and he had to drag that and put it on the-- on the street and bang on it and-- and-- and with other Jews, perform that horrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the-- all the streets were paved with the [? lapids-- ?] is it called in English?-- of all the Jews that had perished and were buried in the cemetery. And the cemetery was completely destroyed in this horrible manner. It was like a Machiavellic-- like a Kafkan book or situation. They-- they invented the most horrible and horrifying things for the Jewish people to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, we were all taken to the [? Janowska ?] prison because my father was declared a saboteur, that he was sabotaging his work. And that was not true. But somehow, somebody said he was guilty of that, and we were-- and they were going to condemn him to be killed. My mother, in desperation, took some of the jewels of the family and run to the-- to the camp, and we were retained there for several days. And at that time, there was no one to talk to. So she went to the commandant and gave him a large diamond in exchange, and he accepted to-- to leave us alone. And so we escaped from that horror.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in the prison camp after she--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --gave the diamond to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went back to the ghetto. We were only allowed to return to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you witnessing acts of anti-Semitism inside the ghetto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beatings, yes, the beatings, the killings. I was witnessing already for several months the destruction of humanity in the vilest ways, but nothing compared to what yet would come. Being as young as we were and thinking that this is the way that life is, as I speak to you here, I-- to me, this is normal. This is my environment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'd begun to believe that this was a normal environment for children to be in and for people to live in. Chaos had-- had become the daily fare, like it had become normal for me to stand in line every morning for a piece of bread for me to eat and a cup of water. And that was all we were able to eat day in and day out in the ghetto. They fed you once a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once a day. There was no more. These were the rations that we were entitled to. Sometimes my mother was able-- she left the ghetto to scrounge up a little bit more food, but very, very seldom. So for me, this was the normal way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And slowly, I kept asking, where is aunt this one or uncle the other, where they were not coming back? They were killed at the [? Janowska ?] prison. Most of the brothers of my mother were killed at the [? Janowska ?] concentration camp, on my mother's family and on my father's family. Some of there were simply shot on the street, and other were started to be carted off to the trains that took them to Treblinka, to Majdanek, and to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: They still had no plans for the children. But soon they decided that it was time-- and it was 1942-- that the children were a hindrance, and they would live or survive maybe and continue the race. And they had orders now to exterminate the Jewish race altogether. In Germany and in the west of Poland, they had already started with the burnings and the massacres. We were in the west-- in the southeast of Poland, so news were coming in on a slower basis. In any event, my mother-- my mother's family, our family, had dwindled to almost none, from the-- we were probably, among cousins and uncles and aunts, the immediate family, probably well over 200 people. Very few had remained alive. And all of them, as they died, left the jewels with the last one. And my mother suddenly found herself with my father and two sisters, no cousins to speak of anymore, no-- nobody left in that little apartment. And so she heard that next day there was going to be a razia for the children. The Germans had decided to liquidate all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she spoke to my father and to her sisters-- they got together-- what to do? There was really no way out. And her sisters advised her to just stay and wait, and maybe a miracle will happen. And my mother said that she was not going to wait for a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why all these stories, and the way I tell it, and if I tell it at all, it is to remember the tremendous sacrifice my mother did. And all my words are for her, to thank her once more for what she did for us. She gave us life once more-- once when we were born, and here, again, she gave us life. And she said to my father and to my aunts, I am not staying here. I am going to go and escape and try to be alive and give life to the children rather than staying here. Because I know if I stay here, I will leap into an open grave, and I will not take my children to a grave. And so that night, they planned. My father was not able to go with her. They decided that he would stay and wait for her to settle herself and see how her escape would go. And then she would somehow go back and get the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on a very cold winter night, she stood by us, took off all the Jewish star, the yellow stars of shame off of us, and said to the children, carry each one of us something. She would carry the baby in her arms, and we would run at night before they closed the ghetto. We would walk out, but we will never come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since our appearance was not the common, denominated at that time, Jewish format-- my mother had green eyes. She had short black hair. My sister was the problem because her hair was brown and curly, and she had more of what they would name a Jewish face. But I was blonde with big braids, green eyes. And the baby, of course, was just a baby. Nobody paid attention to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, you walk first. If they see us, they will look at you first. And if you are asked something-- we were extremely brought up. We never spoke until spoken to. And my mother said, do not open your mouth. And with one eye-- without-- with one look of her eye, she knew how to control us. So she took the Star of David off of us and took us, with a little suitcase and her last fur coat, because of the-- to cover us in-- in the cold, and we went straight to the train station. The train station in [? Limburg ?] was a beautiful, glass-domed station, very similar to the one in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it had poles holding up the sky-- the dome, the glass dome. When we got to the station, of each of those poles hang a person, a Jew, with a big sign saying, this is what happens to the Jews who dare to escape. They are hanged on the spot. And I saw that, and I trembled, and I held onto my mother's hand. And she said, not to be afraid, just walk. She had left behind all the papers, all pictures that might identify us, all our passes, all our ration cards, everything at all that might point a finger to us. She had sewn into her girdle the last of the jewels. And with that, we left the [? Limburger ?] ghetto. And to this day, and 1943 when the ghetto was closed, I believe that we are the only survivors of that-- that were left alive from the [? Limburger ?] ghetto. I have never come across to another person who stayed and survived till the end. We boarded the first train that came without knowing where it was going to. We were on the train for about half an hour when they came to inspect the papers. My mother had nothing. The three of us were lying on the floor-- it was a-- a train for cattle, it was a cattle train-- covered with that fur coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German came. And the train was moving quite fast. The German came. My mother was a young and a beautiful woman. German came and he said, where are your papers? So my mother made like she had lost the papers and she said to him, come over here because I don't want to wake the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: The door was open, and the train was moving fast. She pushed him off the train, because she knew that this would have been the only way she can-- she could save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people in the cattle car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not-- they were-- no. It was at night. They were either sleeping-- nobody saw anything. She made sure of that. And she pushed him off the-- off the train. At the next station, she asked a farmer who was sitting there, please look at my children. I have to go down to buy some milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she went down for five minutes, and then she came back screaming, my purse was stolen, my purse was stolen. Where is my purse? And this woman, this farmer, was there, and she was-- you left it maybe here. And they start looking for her purse, for the documents. And nothing, of course, appeared. So she said to my mother, don't worry, don't worry. Don't get so hysterical. My mother spoke, of course, fluent Polish. And then we-- when the next Germans came later on to look for papers, she was a witness that my mother's purse had been stolen with all the documents. And so the train arrived at a place called Buczacz in the mountain in the south of Poland, and we descended. We got off the train and stood there for a little while, what to do? It was very cold. And my mother said, let us go-- she asked where the priest's house. She was hoping that a man of cloth, a religious man, would be able to help us. And so each little town in Poland has a church, and next to the church lives the-- the parish priest. And he took us in immediately, asked who we were. And my mother told him, we are-- she is the wife of a Polish officer who is fighting at the front. And our house had been bombed-- she mentioned another city-- and everything was burned in it, and we had to leave. And we have no place to go, and won't he please help us? If he knew we were Jewish or not, we never found out. And in this little city, I don't if-- at that time, the pope, who is the pope today, lived. And who knows? Maybe it was him who helped us.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any event, we stayed at the priest's house for a week. And he fed us, and he gave us care. And he was a very, very humane person-- didn't ask any questions. But then he told my mother that in order to settle in this town she needed to have papers. And the only way to get papers was to go to the gendarmerie, to the Germans, and they would interrogate her. But she had nothing to fear, of course. Being the wife of a Polish officer, they would give her the papers that would identify us. When those papers are given, it's very difficult to come by them because the Germans were suspicious. Many Jews were escaping in that manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother left the baby and my oldest sister who looked Jewish with the baby. And she took me, who was the person who most resembled a Christian, and we went to the police station. And the policeman started interrogating my mother. Who are you? What is your name? So we gave false names. My mother's name was-- became Maria [NON-ENGLISH], sounded very Polish. She said that is her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked her to say some prayers in Polish. And she remembered from her religious class some of the prayers, and she recited them for them. And in the end, they brought a bible and ask her to swear on the bible that up to the third generation there was no Jewish blood in her family, which she swore. And that gave her permission to receive the rations and the identification papers. And the priest help her to rent a little house on a hill. The woman who lived there-- very, very Catholic and anti-Semitic-- would rent us one room in her cottage, only one room. And instead of that, she expected my mother to do the cooking and help her out. And my mother took it. And in the meantime, she was established. She had the children together. And now came the form-- the-- the-- the methods that she looked for to bring her sisters and my father out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, were you attending the Catholic church?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. In the meantime, we were to go to church. Everybody in the town, in the village, goes to church once a week, which is on a Sunday morning for mass. So at the beginning, we did not know what was happening, and we made mistakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, my sister asked my mother-- my mother told her, go into the kitchen and bring me a spoon. So my sister said, which one do you want, the one for milk or the one for meat? So my mother started a conversation with this woman, and luckily the woman did not understand what was happened. So we put the situation, our situation, in danger day by day because we were children. That's why the partisans-- the Jews that escaped to the forest, would not take children. They would only take grown-ups.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, the house was nice. We started going to church. Church was very pleasant for me. We were very happy. I-- my sister never wanted to go. She always had a headache. She suffered from migraine headaches. But I was enchanted with the Catholic traditions and-- and prayers and things. And I went as far as being the helper to the priest because I loved it so much. So I waited all week to wear my one pair of shoes and my one whole dress to be able to assist the mass and was very upset with my sister who never wanted to go. And so, as children do, we devised a game with the other children, and we would run after my sister, pointing a finger at her and calling her Jew. This was one of our favorite games. And I could have been the cause of her death. Without understanding what it meant, we were playing this game.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day a German came in, walked into my house and says to my mother, where is the Jew? Where is the Jew? My mother said, there are no Jews here. These are my-- my children. How dare you? How dare-- she could have gone on her knees, begged for mercy like most of them did, frightened and scared. But this shows you her will, her strength. She says, look at my house. She had, on every wall, three saints. She had the pictures of the Christ and Mary and the Virgin. And she said, how dare you offend me in such a manner? Get out of my house. She intimidated him, and he profusely apologized and left.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my mother was able to bring my father out, smuggle him in a hay car. Also, through the jewels, paying, she was able to smuggle him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what kind of a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a hay car.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a hay cart. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Somebody that she knew from [? Limburg, ?] from Lwow, was able to bring him in. And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came out and he said that there was no one left already alive. He was the last one. Because he had been working at the [? Janowska, ?] the labor camp, he survived, and he escaped, hidden. And he had a Jewish accent, so he couldn't live like a Christian, like a Catholic, like a Gentile. And my mother immediately dug-- and the woman was not allowed to know he was there. So she dug a tunnel in the back of our house in the garden at night, every night. And when the tunnel was ready she had him brought in. In.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that wet tunnel in the summer, he lived with blankets, and at night she would give him some food. And in the winter they would bring him in at night. My father stood behind a oven, which was in disuse. And being as religious as he was, he made my mother light the Shabbos candles under the bed every Friday. And the children were not allowed to see him. One day, I was very, very sick. I had strep throat, and I was delirious. And he came out of hiding to sit by me. And it was cold, and he had a scarf. So he took the scarf off, and he wrapped it around my throat to make me feel better. And next day, I asked my mother, who was this lovely gentleman, this precious man who held me in his hands, that I don't remember who he is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother said, no, there was no one here, [NON-ENGLISH]. There was this-- you were sick, and you were imagining things. But I had the scarf around my neck. So here is the scarf, this treasure that I have carried with me wherever I have gone. And I still have the scarf with me to this day. And this is the last item of my father and of his arms around me that I can remember. The pogroms-- the killing started. The Jews in front of us were killed and exterminated right and left. Once a young man, a young boy, was running, and the Germans after him. And he stopped in front of our house, and he was shot. And his blood splattered on the windows of the house, and I was behind the windows. And he fell to the ground begging for water, and my mother came out immediately and brought him some water. And the German came and said, if you feed him, if you give him a drop, I will kill you with him on the spot. Get into your house. So mother had to leave him to die and came in into the house. And another day-- we lived on a hill, as I said. On the bottom of the hill there was a big house in ruins. And in that house, one night a Ukrainian boy discovered voices and went running to the Germans and said, there are Jews hiding there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jews-- the German police came, a whole 20 or 50 of them. And they dug a small hole and they said to the Jews, we know you are there. You either come out or we burn you alive. So--
